children.name: Dasyornis
children.description: A genus of secretive, ground-dwelling passerines endemic to Australia, characterised by the prominent bristles around the base of the bill for which they are named. They are stout, long-tailed birds with strong legs and a furtive nature, inhabiting dense heath, sedgeland, and coastal scrub. Their loud, ringing calls are far easier to hear than the birds themselves are to see.

children.name: Dasyornis brachypterus
children.description: A medium-sized, olive-brown bristlebird with a long, graduated tail and a thin, reedy song, inhabiting dense heathland and forest understorey along the coast of eastern Australia. It spends most of its life on the ground or in low vegetation, foraging for insects and seeds in the leaf litter. Fire management is critical for its conservation, as its habitat is fire-prone and fragmented.

children.name: Dasyornis longirostris
children.description: A slender, rufous-brown bristlebird with a relatively long, slender bill and a loud, flute-like song, restricted to coastal heathlands of southwestern Australia. It is one of the rarest birds in Western Australia, with populations severely impacted by habitat fragmentation and wildfire. Its preferred habitat is dense, old-growth heath with a thick litter layer.

children.name: Dasyornis broadbenti
children.description: The largest and most richly coloured bristlebird, with a deep rufous back, a grey breast, and a long, floppy tail, found in dense coastal scrub from South Australia to Victoria. Its powerful, ringing song carries through the coastal heath, but the bird itself remains frustratingly hidden in dense vegetation. The Coorong subspecies is considered critically endangered.
